text: It is not clear whether the final words of this verse refer to Adam’s original innocence, or to his deliverance from the death-penalty after he had sinned.
This chapter, in the original, mentions no proper names; a few of them have here been supplied, in accordance with modern usage.
‘For Cain’s fault’ is an expression difficult to account for, except on the view that the ‘sons of God’ mentioned in Gen. 6.2, are the descendants of Seth, the ‘children of men’ those of Cain.

The Latin here obscures the sense of the original, by describing the nations as ‘coming together’. According to the Greek text, they ‘were confounded’, almost certainly a reference to Gen. 11.7, where the same verb is used. Mankind, for its sin, was split up into a multitude of nations, but Wisdom saw to it that one of these, with Abraham as its founder, should be different from the rest.

Unless the word ‘infants’ is to be understood metaphorically (of the Jews, as a people newly born by their ransoming from Egypt), this verse seems to preserve a tradition not found in Ex. 15.1, that even little children took part in the song of Moses.
